nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, clear, slow-moving to moderately flowing rivers, streams, and tributaries; associated with submerged wood, leaf litter, and root tangles in well-oxygenated, dimly lit stretches
nativeRange.biotope: South American riverine — clear-water blackwater and whitewater tributaries with submerged driftwood and decaying leaf litter

decorPreferences: driftwood — multiple pieces with biofilm (aufwuchs) for grazing surfaces
broad-leaved plants (Amazon swords, Anubias) for additional grazing and resting surfaces
low to subdued lighting to encourage daytime activity
leaf litter (Indian almond, catappa) for natural biotope feel

diet.notes: Obligate aufwuchs (biofilm/algae) grazer. In the aquarium, the primary food source should be biofilm and soft algae grazed from driftwood and flat surfaces; supplement with algae wafers, blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, courgette, spinach, lettuce), spirulina-based foods, and sinking herbivore pellets. Occasional protein via small amounts of frozen bloodworm or brine shrimp may be accepted but plant matter must dominate. A well-established tank with abundant driftwood biofilm is essential — newly-set-up tanks without adequate biofilm growth are a common cause of starvation. Avoid copper-based medications.

goodWith: small, peaceful tetras (cardinal tetras, neon tetras, rummy-nose tetras)
dwarf corydoras (not competing for identical substrate zones)
small peaceful dwarf cichlids (Apistogramma spp.) if tank is large enough
avoidWith: fin-nipping species (tiger barbs, serpae tetras) — long twig-like body and fins are vulnerable
boisterous or fast-moving large fish that cause stress
other suckermouth catfish competing aggressively for territory (large plecos, hypostomus)
cichlids that are territorial or disruptive to substrate
copper-based medications lethal to this species

summary: Genus-level entry covering the South American twig catfish group (Loricariidae). Remarkably stick-shaped aufwuchs grazers requiring mature, driftwood-rich tanks with established biofilm. Peaceful and delicate; intermediate care due to strict water quality and dietary requirements.
careNotes:
Farlowella spp. (twig catfish, stick catfish) are elongated, remarkably twig-shaped suckermouth catfish from slow-moving South American rivers, where they cling to submerged wood and vegetation grazing biofilm and algae. Their camouflage is superb — in a well-decorated tank they are almost indistinguishable from actual twigs. This genus-level entry covers the multiple Farlowella species sold under the common names "twig catfish" and "stick catfish" in the hobby; Farlowella acus is among the most frequently traded. MERGE CANDIDATE: fw-fish-343 "Farlowella" (Farlowella acus) covers the same species group and should be reviewed for consolidation.

Tank maturity is the single most important husbandry factor. Twig catfish must not be added to newly-established aquaria — they depend on a thick coating of biofilm (aufwuchs) on driftwood and flat surfaces, and will starve in bare or new tanks even if algae wafers are offered. A tank of at least 6–12 months maturity with multiple pieces of aged driftwood is strongly advised. Regular driftwood and smooth-rock surfaces are essential grazing territories; replace or rotate pieces if biofilm appears depleted.

Water quality must be pristine. Farlowella are sensitive to elevated nitrates and ammonia; weekly water changes of 25–30% are standard. Maintain soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–12). Temperature stability is important; avoid rapid swings. Moderate flow and high oxygenation reflect their natural fast-stream microhabitats. Avoid any copper-based treatments — this family (Loricariidae) is highly copper-sensitive.

Daytime activity is limited; most feeding occurs at night or in very dim conditions. Subdued lighting or a planted, shaded aquarium encourages more visible behaviour. Males develop elongated rostral odontodes (cheek bristles) at maturity. Keep individually or in pairs (1 male, 1 female); males may show territorial behaviour toward conspecifics in smaller tanks. Do not house with fin-nippers — the elongated body profile and slow movement make them easy targets.
breedingNotes: Farlowella breed in captivity when conditions are optimal; success is considered moderately difficult. A well-established tank, high water quality, slight temperature increase (to 26–27°C), and increased feeding of fresh vegetables and spirulina are typical triggers. Males clean a spawning site — usually a flat, smooth surface such as a broad plant leaf, slate, or smooth piece of driftwood — and the female deposits 40–60 adhesive eggs in a single row or cluster. The male guards and fans the eggs until hatching at approximately 7–10 days at 25°C. Fry are miniature versions of adults and require established biofilm surfaces plus fine algae wafers crushed to powder; infusoria or micro-worms can supplement early feeding. Fry are fragile and susceptible to water quality lapses. Remove the male once fry are free-swimming to prevent accidental egg disturbance.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Farlowella acus
primary.notes: Primary source for species biology, water parameters (temperature 23–27°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 36–215 ppm / ~2–12 dH), adult size, natural range, diet (aufwuchs grazer), breeding behaviour (male egg-guard, flat surface spawning, 7–10 day hatch), and husbandry notes including mature-tank requirement and copper sensitivity.

additional.name: FishBase — Farlowella acus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), adult size (to ~20 cm TL reported for larger species in genus), native distribution (Venezuela, Trinidad, Colombia, Brazil), and diet classification (herbivore/grazer). FishBase lists multiple Farlowella species with overlapping ranges.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Twig Catfish Care
additional.notes: Hobbyist-trade care guide; confirmed mature-tank requirement, driftwood biofilm dependency, blanched-vegetable diet, peaceful temperament, fin-nipper avoidance, and breeding overview (flat-surface egg deposition, paternal guard). Tank size recommendation of 29+ gallons (approximately 110 L) cited.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Farlowella species profile
additional.notes: Supplementary husbandry source; confirmed aufwuchs grazing ecology, slow-water biotope, water-quality sensitivity, and comparison with related Sturisoma genus. Noted importance of aged driftwood and algae-encrusted surfaces and advises against housing with boisterous or fin-nipping tankmates.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeds in captivity in mature, well-maintained tanks. Male guards adhesive eggs on flat surfaces (slate, broad leaves, smooth wood); hatching at 7–10 days at 25°C. Fry require fine biofilm and powdered algae-based foods. Moderate difficulty due to mature-tank prerequisite and fry sensitivity to water quality.
